---
title: "Maximum Likelihood & GMM"
subtitle: "The estimation machinery beneath every model"
---

## Overview

Ordinary least squares is one estimator among many, and it answers only a narrow question: the conditional mean of a continuous outcome. The moment we move to probabilities, counts, durations, or models defined by economic theory, we need a more general principle for turning data into parameter estimates. Two such principles dominate modern econometrics.

**Maximum likelihood** asks: which parameter values make the observed data most probable? It is the workhorse behind logit, probit, Poisson, duration, and mixed models, and it comes with a complete and elegant large-sample theory --- consistency, asymptotic normality, and standard errors that fall directly out of the curvature of the likelihood.

**The generalized method of moments** asks instead: which parameter values make a set of theoretical moment conditions hold in the data? GMM is the unifying language of much of applied micro- and macroeconometrics; OLS and instrumental variables are both special cases.

This module builds both from first principles, develops the asymptotic theory that makes inference possible, and ends with the three classical tests --- likelihood ratio, Wald, and Lagrange multiplier --- that you will use to test hypotheses in every nonlinear model that follows.

## Pages in This Module

- [The Likelihood Principle](likelihood.qmd) --- Finding the parameters that make the data most probable.
- [Properties and Asymptotics of MLE](properties-asymptotics.qmd) --- Consistency, asymptotic normality, and the information matrix.
- [Method of Moments and GMM](method-of-moments-gmm.qmd) --- Estimation from moment conditions.
- [The Classical Trinity: LR, Wald, and LM Tests](likelihood-inference.qmd) --- Three roads to the same hypothesis test.
